title: Lab 2 · case_when() — Multi-tier Recode
scenario: HR wants to classify staff by seniority based on their hourly rate to plan training budgets.
task:
Using case_when() inside mutate(), create Experience_Level:
  • Hourly_Rate < 22 → "Junior"
  • 22 ≤ Hourly_Rate < 30 → "Mid"
  • Hourly_Rate ≥ 30 → "Senior"
Convert the result to a factor.
starter:
library(dplyr)
Name <- c("Tane","Aroha","Liam","Mia","Connor","Priya")
Role <- as.factor(c("Barista","Manager","Barista","Cashier","Manager","Cashier"))
Hourly_Rate <- c(22,35,20,21,33,22)
Hours_Worked <- c(38,40,15,25,42,30)
cafe <- data.frame(Name,Role,Hourly_Rate,Hours_Worked)
cafe <- cafe %>% mutate(Weekly_Pay=Hourly_Rate*Hours_Worked, Annual_Pay=Weekly_Pay*52,
                        Pay_Level=as.factor(ifelse(Weekly_Pay>=600,"High","Low")))

# Add Experience_Level
cafe <- cafe %>% mutate(
  Experience_Level = case_when(
    
  ),
  Experience_Level = as.factor(Experience_Level)
)
cafe

title: Lab 2 · select() — Column Subset
scenario: The payroll report only needs 4 columns. Extract just those from the full dataset.
task:
Select only: Name, Weekly_Pay, Pay_Level, Experience_Level

Do it two ways:
(a) Bracket notation: cafe[ , c(...)]
(b) dplyr: cafe %>% select(...)
starter:
library(dplyr)
Name <- c("Tane","Aroha","Liam","Mia","Connor","Priya")
Role <- as.factor(c("Barista","Manager","Barista","Cashier","Manager","Cashier"))
Hourly_Rate <- c(22,35,20,21,33,22)
Hours_Worked <- c(38,40,15,25,42,30)
cafe <- data.frame(Name,Role,Hourly_Rate,Hours_Worked)
cafe <- cafe %>% mutate(Weekly_Pay=Hourly_Rate*Hours_Worked,Annual_Pay=Weekly_Pay*52,
  Pay_Level=as.factor(ifelse(Weekly_Pay>=600,"High","Low")),
  Experience_Level=as.factor(case_when(Hourly_Rate<22~"Junior",Hourly_Rate<30~"Mid",TRUE~"Senior")))

# (a) Bracket notation

# (b) select()

title: Lab 2 · BONUS — Complex Filtering & Bonus Pay
scenario: Final challenge combining everything from Lab 2. Three separate tasks to complete.
task:
(a) Filter: Show rows where Pay_Level == "High" AND Role != "Manager"

(b) Calculate the average Weekly_Pay — expected answer: 684.5

(c) Add a Bonus column: 10% of Weekly_Pay for Senior, 5% for everyone else. Select Name, Experience_Level, Weekly_Pay, Bonus.
starter:
library(dplyr)
Name <- c("Tane","Aroha","Liam","Mia","Connor","Priya")
Role <- as.factor(c("Barista","Manager","Barista","Cashier","Manager","Cashier"))
Hourly_Rate <- c(22,35,20,21,33,22)
Hours_Worked <- c(38,40,15,25,42,30)
cafe <- data.frame(Name,Role,Hourly_Rate,Hours_Worked)
cafe <- cafe %>% mutate(Weekly_Pay=Hourly_Rate*Hours_Worked,Annual_Pay=Weekly_Pay*52,
  Pay_Level=as.factor(ifelse(Weekly_Pay>=600,"High","Low")),
  Experience_Level=as.factor(case_when(Hourly_Rate<22~"Junior",Hourly_Rate<30~"Mid",TRUE~"Senior")))

# (a) High Pay AND NOT Manager

# (b) Average Weekly Pay

# (c) Add Bonus and select columns

title: Monte Carlo — Basic Setup
scenario: You are building a Monte Carlo simulation in Excel to model Emily's superannuation growth under uncertainty. Emily earns $60,000/year with a standard deviation of $8,000.
task:
Setup steps:
  1. In cell B1: mean salary = 60000
  2. In cell B2: std dev = 8000
  3. In A2:A1001, enter the formula to generate 1,000 random normal draws:
    =NORM.INV(RAND(), $B$1, $B$2)
  4. Press F9 to recalculate and observe the distribution changing
starter:
# Formula notes (write the key Excel formulas here)
# Monte Carlo in A2:A1001
formula <- "=NORM.INV(RAND(), $B$1, $B$2)"

# Check mean of simulation (in Excel: =AVERAGE(A2:A1001))
# Check std dev: =STDEV(A2:A1001)

title: Scenario Analysis — IF & Named Cells
scenario: Emily's growth rate varies by economic scenario. Model three scenarios with Excel formulas.
task:
In Excel:
  1. Cell B5: type scenario label ("base", "pessimistic", or "optimistic")
  2. Base return: =B2*1.02
  3. Pessimistic: =B2*0.98
  4. Optimistic: =B2*1.05
  5. Combine with IF() nesting or SWITCH()
starter:
# Note the Excel IF logic here:
# =IF(B5="pessimistic", B2*0.98,
#    IF(B5="optimistic", B2*1.05, B2*1.02))

# Or using SWITCH:
# =SWITCH(B5, "pessimistic", B2*0.98,
#             "optimistic",  B2*1.05,
#             B2*1.02)

title: Glide Path — Dynamic Asset Allocation
scenario: Emily is 30 years old. As she ages, her portfolio should shift from high-growth (SkyHigh) to stable (HarbourSafe) funds — a strategy called a Glide Path.
task:
Build a Glide Path table (rows = age 30 to 65):
  1. Column A: Age 30 to 65
  2. Column B — SkyHigh %: =MAX(0, 1 - (A2-30)/35)
  3. Column C — HarbourSafe %: =1-B2
  4. Column D — Expected return: =B2*0.09 + C2*0.04
  5. Column E — Portfolio value: starts at $50,000, grows by return + $5,000 annual contribution
starter:
# Glide Path key formulas:
# SkyHigh allocation: =MAX(0, 1-(A2-30)/35)
# HarbourSafe:        =1-B2
# Expected return:    =B2*0.09 + C2*0.04
# Portfolio value:    =E1*(1+D2) + 5000

# Simulate in R:
ages <- 30:65
skyhigh <- pmax(0, 1 - (ages - 30)/35)
harboursafe <- 1 - skyhigh
expected_return <- skyhigh * 0.09 + harboursafe * 0.04
data.frame(Age = ages, SkyHigh = round(skyhigh,2),
           HarbourSafe = round(harboursafe,2),
           Return = round(expected_return,4))
